It is high by American standards. It is not high by the standards of successful democracies. And critically, it applies in a world where GDP has approximately doubled: the absolute tax burden per dollar of output is higher, but the absolute income after taxes is also substantially higher than today. A household earning $142,000 in a 41 percent tax-to-GDP economy has more purchasing power than a household earning $83,730 in a 27 percent tax-to-GDP economy.

## 3.4  Path Dependency: The Transition Is the Hard Part

The preceding section presents the destination. This section addresses the journey—and it is here that the analysis becomes most consequential for policy. The end-state math is encouraging. The transition math is sobering.

***The Bridge Problem***

Capital income requires large asset stocks. Large asset stocks require decades of compounding. But wages can decline significantly within a single decade if automation accelerates. This creates a **temporal mismatch** that all three models identified independently: the new income source (capital returns) is not mature when the old income source (wages) begins to contract. There is a gap. During that gap, transfers must spike to maintain purchasing power and prevent the aggregate demand collapse described in Part One.

Each model quantified this bridge differently, but the structural finding was identical:

**ChatGPT 5.2 Pro **modeled universal cash transfers peaking at approximately $36,000 per household per year around year sixteen of the transition, with total government fiscal capture peaking at approximately 29 percent of GDP around year twenty—before declining as sovereign wealth fund dividends and other capital returns began to displace tax-funded transfers.

**Claude Opus 4.6 **identified Phase 3 (2035–2045) as the critical stress period, with UBI carrying the heaviest bridge load at approximately 10 percent of GDP—roughly $4.3 to $6.9 trillion per year at projected GDP levels.

**Grok 4.20 **showed transfers doing the heavy lifting from 2025 through 2045, then their growth rate slowing dramatically as capital income matured and began replacing transfer-funded purchasing power.

The bridge is not optional. If transfers do not expand to fill the gap while capital accumulates, aggregate demand drops during the transition even if the end state is viable. The system must spend money it has not yet earned from capital returns—financed by taxation during the build-up period—in order to maintain the consumer demand that keeps the economy functioning while the ownership infrastructure matures.

***Three Transition Paths***

To test the sensitivity of outcomes to policy timing, Claude’s analysis modeled three distinct transition paths, varying the start date and aggressiveness of the policy response. The results, tracked at five-year intervals over a thirty-five-year horizon, are presented below.

The divergence between paths is not linear. It is **exponential**. The proactive path, beginning sovereign wealth fund capitalization in 2025, reaches $7.2 trillion by 2035. The delayed path, beginning around 2035, reaches only $9.1 trillion by 2045—a full decade later and at roughly the same scale the proactive path achieved ten years earlier. By 2060, the proactive fund holds $132.2 trillion; the delayed fund holds $68.8 trillion. Starting ten years late does not cost ten years of growth. It costs nearly half the total accumulation, because the lost years are the early compounding years when the growth curve is steepest in percentage terms.

***The Valley of Death***

The delayed path produces a trough—a period between approximately 2037 and 2043 where median household income falls to approximately **$83,000 to $87,000**, barely above today’s level despite substantially higher GDP. This is not because the economy is smaller. It is because the economy is producing far more, but the income channels connecting that production to household purchasing power have been severed (wages declining) without replacement channels being ready (capital not yet mature, transfers insufficient). Output is abundant; demand is absent. This is the deflationary scenario described in Part One, manifesting not as a theoretical risk but as a concrete projection.

The political implications of the trough are severe. A period of fifteen to twenty years during which living standards stagnate or decline despite visible technological progress is a recipe for populist backlash, institutional distrust, and policy reversal—precisely the dynamics that could prevent the delayed path from ever reaching its eventual convergence. The delayed path does eventually produce positive outcomes: by 2060, median income reaches $158,000. But the families who lived through the trough years spent two decades earning dramatically less than they could have earned under proactive sequencing.

The compound interest curve is both the most powerful tool in this framework and the most unforgiving constraint. It rewards early action exponentially and punishes delay exponentially. Every year that sovereign wealth fund capitalization is postponed is a year of compounding that can never be recovered.

***The Four-Phase Transition Sequence***

Under the proactive path, the transition unfolds in four phases, each with distinct policy priorities, fiscal characteristics, and structural dynamics:

**Table 3.9: Four-Phase Transition Sequence (Proactive Path)**
